---
title: "Reading the Math"
subtitle: "A Notation Primer for ISLP — Scalars, Vectors, Hats, and Greeks"
author: "Ho-min Park & Hyun-Hwan Jeong"
date: 2026-07-17
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, custom.scss]
    width: 1920
    height: 1080
    margin: 0.08
    slide-number: c/t
    center: true
    transition: fade
    footer: "ML Summer Class · Notation Primer"
    highlight-style: github
    code-line-numbers: false
    html-math-method: katex
    include-in-header:
      - text: |
          <link rel="stylesheet" href="https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/font-awesome/6.5.1/css/all.min.css">
---

# Why This Deck Exists {.divider background-color="#1b3a5c"}

## Math notation is a compressed language

An equation like $\hat{\beta} = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}$
is one short sentence — *if* you can read it. If you can't, it's a wall.

- ISLP assumes fluency in this language but never teaches it explicitly
- This deck is the [pocket dictionary]{.hl}: every symbol convention the
  textbook uses, in one place
- Keep it open next to any chapter — nothing here is more than
  high-school algebra wearing formal clothes

::: notes
Standalone reference deck — go through it before/alongside Weeks 1–2, and
come back whenever a symbol looks unfamiliar.
:::

# The Objects: Scalars, Vectors, Matrices {.divider background-color="#1b3a5c"}

## Scalars: single numbers

Written as *italic* letters: $n$, $p$, $x$, $y$, $\lambda$.

Two scalars are so common in ISLP they're practically reserved words:

- $n$ — the [number of observations]{.hl} (rows of data)
- $p$ — the [number of predictors]{.hl} (input variables)

::: {.fragment}
So "$n = 200$, $p = 3$" in the `Advertising` data means: 200 markets,
each described by 3 ad budgets (TV, radio, newspaper).
:::

## Vectors: ordered lists of numbers

A vector collects several scalars into one object:

$$x = \begin{pmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ \vdots \\ x_p \end{pmatrix}$$

- By convention a vector is a **column** (stacked vertically)
- Its **dimension** is how many entries it has — here $p$
- $x_j$ (one subscript) picks out the $j$-th entry — a scalar again

## When is a vector bold? ISLP's house rule

Most linear algebra texts bold *every* vector ($\mathbf{v}$). ISLP is pickier:

| Notation | Meaning | Length |
|---|---|---|
| $\mathbf{y}$, $\mathbf{a}$ (bold lowercase) | a vector with [one entry per **observation**]{.hl} | $n$ |
| $x_i$, $\beta$ (plain italic) | any other vector — e.g. one observation's features, or the coefficients | $p$ (or $p+1$) |
| $\mathbf{X}$ (bold capital) | a matrix | $n \times p$ |

::: {.fragment}
So boldness is a hint about *length*: bold = "as long as the dataset".
That's why the response $\mathbf{y}$ is bold but a single observation $x_i$
is not — and why you'll see both in the same equation. Other books bold
everything; don't let the font-change throw you when switching sources.
:::

## Matrices: grids of numbers

A matrix is written as a bold capital letter, with its size as
$\text{rows} \times \text{columns}$:

$$\mathbf{X} = \begin{pmatrix} x_{11} & x_{12} & \cdots & x_{1p} \\ x_{21} & x_{22} & \cdots & x_{2p} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ x_{n1} & x_{n2} & \cdots & x_{np} \end{pmatrix} \quad (n \times p)$$

- $x_{ij}$ — the entry in **row $i$, column $j$**: always row first
- A vector is just a matrix with one column ($n \times 1$)
- A scalar is just a $1 \times 1$ matrix — it's objects all the way down

## The data matrix: one grid, two readings

In ISLP, $\mathbf{X}$ is *the dataset itself* — and you can slice it two ways:

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column style="width:50%;"}
### By row
$x_i$ = observation $i$: one market, one patient, one customer — a vector
of its $p$ measurements.

*"For observation $i$…"*
:::
::: {.column style="width:50%;"}
### By column
$\mathbf{x}_j$ = variable $j$: all $n$ values of one predictor, e.g. every
market's TV budget.

*"The predictor $X_j$…"*
:::
::::

::: {.fragment}
When ISLP writes the model $Y = f(X) + \epsilon$, that $X$ means "the
predictors" abstractly — not a specific number. Which brings us to…
:::

## Capital vs lowercase: random vs realized

- **Capital** $X$, $Y$ — the **random variable**: the quantity *in general*,
  before we've measured anything ("income", "whether a customer defaults")
- **Lowercase** $x_i$, $y_i$ — the **observed value** for observation $i$:
  an actual number in your dataset ($x_3 = 41{,}000$)

::: {.fragment}
That's why the model is stated as $Y = f(X) + \epsilon$ (a claim about the
world), but the fitted line is $\hat{y}_i = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 x_i$
(arithmetic on your data).
:::

## Check yourself #1

::: {.quiz}
For the `Advertising` data ($n = 200$ markets, $p = 3$ ad channels),
classify each symbol — scalar, vector, or matrix? What does it refer to?

$$n \qquad \mathbf{X} \qquad x_i \qquad x_{ij} \qquad Y \qquad y_7$$
:::

::: {.fragment}
- $n$ — scalar, the number 200
- $\mathbf{X}$ — the $200 \times 3$ matrix of all ad budgets
- $x_i$ — vector, the 3 budgets of market $i$
- $x_{ij}$ — scalar, market $i$'s spend on channel $j$
- $Y$ — random variable, "sales" in general
- $y_7$ — scalar, the actual sales of market 7
:::

# The Decorations: Sub, Super, Hat {.divider background-color="#1b3a5c"}

## Subscripts: *which one?*

A subscript **indexes** — it answers "which one out of many":

- $x_i$ — the $i$-th **observation** (rows: $i = 1, \dots, n$)
- $\beta_j$ — the coefficient of the $j$-th **predictor** (columns: $j = 1, \dots, p$)
- $x_{ij}$ — both at once: observation $i$, predictor $j$

::: {.fragment}
ISLP is consistent: [$i$ runs over observations, $j$ (or $k$) runs over
variables or classes]{.hl}. When you see a subscript, first ask: is this
counting rows, columns, or classes?
:::

::: {.fragment}
Note the house rule at work: $x_i$ (row $i$, length $p$) stays **plain**,
while $\mathbf{x}_j$ (column $j$, length $n$) is **bold** — same letter,
and only the subscript letter plus the boldness tell you which slice of
$\mathbf{X}$ you're holding.
:::

## Superscripts: usually a power, sometimes a label

- $x^2$, $\texttt{horsepower}^2$ — a **power**: plain squaring
- $\mathbf{X}^T$ — **transpose**: flip rows and columns
  (an $n \times p$ matrix becomes $p \times n$)
- $\mathbf{A}^{-1}$ — **matrix inverse**: the matrix analogue of $\frac{1}{a}$;
  $\mathbf{A}^{-1}\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{I}$ (the identity, matrix-land's "1")
- $R^2$ — just a name with a square in it (it *is* a squared correlation)

::: {.fragment}
$T$ and $-1$ are **operations**, not powers. If a superscript is a letter or
sits on a matrix, suspect an operation.
:::

## The hat: "our estimate of"

The single most important decoration in this course:

$$\beta_1 \;\; \text{vs} \;\; \hat{\beta}_1 \qquad\qquad f \;\; \text{vs} \;\; \hat{f} \qquad\qquad y_i \;\; \text{vs} \;\; \hat{y}_i$$

- Plain symbol — the **true, unknown** quantity (nature's secret)
- Hatted symbol — our **estimate** of it, computed from training data

::: {.fragment}
$y_i$ vs $\hat{y}_i$ deserves care: $y_i$ is what *actually happened*,
$\hat{y}_i$ is what our model *predicted*. Their gap $y_i - \hat{y}_i$ is the
**residual** — the quantity least squares minimizes.
:::

## Bars, tildes, and stars

- $\bar{x}$ — the **sample mean**: $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n x_i$
- $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ — the tilde means ["is distributed as"]{.hl}:
  $X$ follows a normal distribution with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$
- $x^*$ or $x_0$ — a **new, unseen** point at which we want a prediction
  (test point), as opposed to the training points $x_1, \dots, x_n$

## Check yourself #2

::: {.quiz}
Translate each into words:

$$\hat{\beta}_1 \qquad \bar{y} \qquad \mathbf{X}^T \qquad x_{52}^2 \qquad \epsilon \sim N(0, \sigma^2) \qquad \hat{f}(x_0)$$
:::

::: {.fragment}
- $\hat{\beta}_1$ — our estimate of the first coefficient
- $\bar{y}$ — the average of the observed responses
- $\mathbf{X}^T$ — the data matrix, transposed
- $x_{52}^2$ — the square of observation 5's value on predictor 2 (context
  disambiguates 5,2 vs 52!)
- $\epsilon \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$ — the noise is normally distributed, mean 0, variance $\sigma^2$
- $\hat{f}(x_0)$ — our fitted model's prediction at the new point $x_0$
:::

# The Greek Cast {.divider background-color="#1b3a5c"}

## Greek letters you'll meet every week

| Letter | Name | Typical role in ISLP |
|---|---|---|
| $\beta$ | beta | regression **coefficients** — the stars of Ch. 3–4 |
| $\epsilon$ | epsilon | the irreducible **noise** in $Y = f(X) + \epsilon$ |
| $\mu$ | mu | a **mean** |
| $\sigma, \sigma^2$ | sigma | standard deviation / **variance** |
| $\lambda$ | lambda | **tuning knob** — regularization strength (Ch. 6) |
| $\pi_k$ | pi | **prior probability** of class $k$ (Ch. 4 — not 3.14!) |
| $\theta$ | theta | a **generic parameter** when no specific letter applies |
| $\rho$ | rho | **correlation** |
| $\delta_k$ | delta | **discriminant score** of class $k$ (LDA/QDA) |

## The unwritten rule: Greek = unknown, Roman = data

- **Roman letters** ($x$, $y$, $n$, $p$) — things you can *see*: your data
  and its dimensions
- **Greek letters** ($\beta$, $\mu$, $\sigma$) — things you *can't* see:
  the parameters of the process that generated the data
- **Greek with a hat** ($\hat{\beta}$, $\hat{\mu}$) — your data-based
  *guess* at the invisible thing

::: {.fragment}
This is why statisticians write $\hat{\beta}$ rather than $b$: the notation
itself reminds you that [an estimate exists only in relation to an unknown
truth]{.hl}.
:::

## One symbol, two jobs: $\Sigma$ and $\Pi$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i = x_1 + x_2 + \cdots + x_n \qquad\qquad \prod_{i=1}^{n} p_i = p_1 \times p_2 \times \cdots \times p_n$$

Capital sigma = **repeated addition**; capital pi = **repeated
multiplication** (you met $\prod$ in the logistic likelihood).

::: {.fragment}
But in Ch. 4, $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ (bold, no limits attached) is the
**covariance matrix** of a multivariate normal. Same glyph, different job —
[the limits above and below are the giveaway]{.hl}: with limits it's a sum,
without them it's a matrix.
:::

## Check yourself #3

::: {.quiz}
From Week 2's slides — name each Greek letter and its role:

$$p(X) = \frac{e^{\beta_0 + \beta_1 X}}{1 + e^{\beta_0 + \beta_1 X}} \qquad\qquad \Pr(Y = k \mid X = x) = \frac{\pi_k f_k(x)}{\sum_{l=1}^K \pi_l f_l(x)}$$
:::

::: {.fragment}
- $\beta_0, \beta_1$ — betas: intercept and slope on the *log-odds* scale
- $\pi_k$ — pi: the prior probability of class $k$
- $\sum_{l=1}^K$ — capital sigma *with limits*: sum over all $K$ classes
- Bonus: $e$ is Roman — Euler's number 2.718…, a known constant, not a
  parameter
:::

# Functions and Parameters {.divider background-color="#1b3a5c"}

## Function notation: $f$ vs $f(x)$

A function is a **machine**: feed in an input, get an output.

- $f$ — the machine itself (the relationship, the "true pattern")
- $f(x)$ — the machine's **output** at a particular input $x$ — a number
- $\hat{f}$ — our estimated copy of the machine; $\hat{f}(x_0)$ — its
  prediction at a new point

::: {.fragment}
"Estimating $f$" — the phrase defining this whole course — means building a
machine whose outputs mimic nature's, [without ever seeing nature's
blueprints]{.hl}.
:::

## Arguments vs parameters

Two kinds of things live inside a function's parentheses:

- **Arguments** — the inputs that vary: the $X$ in $f(X)$
- **Parameters** — the settings that define *which* function you have:
  the $\beta$'s in $\beta_0 + \beta_1 X$

::: {.fragment}
$p(X)$ in logistic regression is read "the probability, *as a function of*
$X$" — its shape is controlled by parameters $\beta_0, \beta_1$ hiding inside.
Fitting = [using data to choose parameter values]{.hl}; predicting =
plugging arguments into the fitted function.
:::

## Operators: $E[\cdot]$, $\mathrm{Var}(\cdot)$, $\Pr(\cdot)$

These look like functions, and they are — their input is a *random quantity*:

- $E[Y]$ — the **expected value** (long-run average) of $Y$
- $\mathrm{Var}(\hat{f}(x_0))$ — the **variance**: how much this quantity
  would wobble across different training sets
- $\Pr(Y = k \mid X = x)$ — a **conditional probability**: the chance $Y$
  is class $k$, [given that]{.hl} we know $X = x$ — the "$\mid$" is read
  "given"

::: {.fragment}
None of these are multiplications: $E[Y]$ is not "$E$ times $Y$". Brackets
after an operator name mean "applied to".
:::

## Compact shorthands worth memorizing

- $I(\text{condition})$ — the **indicator**: equals 1 if the condition holds,
  0 if not. Week 1's error rate $\frac{1}{n}\sum_i I(y_i \neq \hat{y}_i)$
  literally says: *count the mistakes, divide by $n$*
- $\arg\min_\beta \; g(\beta)$ — "the value of $\beta$ that makes $g$
  smallest" ($\arg\max$: largest). Least squares in one line:
  $\hat{\beta} = \arg\min_\beta \mathrm{RSS}(\beta)$
- $\log$ — in ISLP (and most of ML), always the **natural log** (base $e$)
- $\mathbb{R}^p$ — the set of all vectors with $p$ real entries; "$x \in
  \mathbb{R}^p$" = "$x$ is a $p$-dimensional vector"

# Worked Example: Simple Linear Regression, Three Ways {.divider background-color="#1b3a5c"}

## The model, symbol by symbol

$$y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i + \epsilon_i, \qquad i = 1, \dots, n$$

- $y_i$ — observed response of observation $i$ (Roman, lowercase: data)
- $x_i$ — observed predictor of observation $i$ (data)
- $\beta_0, \beta_1$ — unknown intercept and slope (Greek: parameters)
- $\epsilon_i$ — observation $i$'s unobservable noise (Greek: unknown)
- The trailing "$i = 1, \dots, n$" means: [this equation holds $n$ times,
  once per observation]{.hl} — it's $n$ equations wearing one line

## A dataset small enough to see everything

Three observations ($n = 3$, $p = 1$) — think "TV spend vs sales":

| $i$ | $x_i$ | $y_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 2 |
| 2 | 2 | 4 |
| 3 | 3 | 3 |

The sample means (bars!): $\bar{x} = \frac{1+2+3}{3} = 2$, $\quad \bar{y} = \frac{2+4+3}{3} = 3$.

::: notes
Numbers chosen so every later computation comes out in clean fractions —
follow along by hand, that's the point of this section.
:::

## Way 1: scalar arithmetic

The Ch. 3 least squares formulas, then plug in:

$$\hat{\beta}_1 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^n (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^n (x_i - \bar{x})^2} = \frac{(-1)(-1) + (0)(1) + (1)(0)}{(-1)^2 + 0^2 + 1^2} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$\hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{y} - \hat{\beta}_1 \bar{x} = 3 - \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 2 = 2$$

::: {.fragment}
Fitted line: $\hat{y} = 2 + 0.5x$. Notice the notation working: sums run
over $i$ (observations), bars are means, hats appear exactly when data
replaces truth.
:::

## Way 2: the same data as vectors and a matrix

Stack the $n$ scalar equations into one vector equation
$\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{X}\beta + \epsilon$:

$$\underbrace{\begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 4 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}}_{\mathbf{y}\;(3 \times 1)} = \underbrace{\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \end{pmatrix}}_{\mathbf{X}\;(3 \times 2)} \underbrace{\begin{pmatrix} \beta_0 \\ \beta_1 \end{pmatrix}}_{\beta\;(2 \times 1)} + \underbrace{\begin{pmatrix} \epsilon_1 \\ \epsilon_2 \\ \epsilon_3 \end{pmatrix}}_{\epsilon\;(3 \times 1)}$$

- The column of 1's is the intercept's seat — it multiplies $\beta_0$
- $\mathbf{X}$ here is called the **design matrix**: one row per
  observation, one column per parameter

## Multiplying it out: the two notations are the same thing

Matrix × vector = take each **row** of $\mathbf{X}$, multiply entrywise with
$\beta$, add up:

$$\mathbf{X}\beta = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \cdot \beta_0 + 1 \cdot \beta_1 \\ 1 \cdot \beta_0 + 2 \cdot \beta_1 \\ 1 \cdot \beta_0 + 3 \cdot \beta_1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot 1 \\ \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot 2 \\ \beta_0 + \beta_1 \cdot 3 \end{pmatrix}$$

Row $i$ is exactly the scalar equation $\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i$.
[The matrix form isn't new math — it's the same three equations,
holding hands.]{.hl}

## Way 3: the one-line solution

$$\hat{\beta} = (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}$$

Reading right to left, with our numbers:

$$\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 6 \\ 6 & 14 \end{pmatrix} \qquad \mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y} = \begin{pmatrix} 9 \\ 19 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\hat{\beta} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 6 \\ 6 & 14 \end{pmatrix}^{-1} \begin{pmatrix} 9 \\ 19 \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{6}\begin{pmatrix} 14 & -6 \\ -6 & 3 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 9 \\ 19 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 0.5 \end{pmatrix}$$

::: {.fragment}
Same $\hat{\beta}_0 = 2$, $\hat{\beta}_1 = 0.5$ as the scalar route — and
this is [literally what `statsmodels` computes]{.hl} when you call `.fit()`,
for any number of predictors.
:::

## Predictions and residuals, both ways

$$\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{X}\hat{\beta} = \begin{pmatrix} 2.5 \\ 3 \\ 3.5 \end{pmatrix} \qquad \mathbf{e} = \mathbf{y} - \hat{\mathbf{y}} = \begin{pmatrix} -0.5 \\ 1 \\ -0.5 \end{pmatrix}$$

$$\mathrm{RSS} = \sum_{i=1}^{3} e_i^2 = 0.25 + 1 + 0.25 = 1.5 \qquad \text{or} \qquad \mathrm{RSS} = \mathbf{e}^T\mathbf{e} = 1.5$$

$\mathbf{e}^T\mathbf{e}$ — a row vector times a column vector — *is* the
sum of squares. Every $\Sigma$-formula in ISLP has a compact matrix twin.

## Check yourself #4

::: {.quiz}
Dimension bookkeeping — if $\mathbf{X}$ is $n \times p$ and $\mathbf{y}$ is
$n \times 1$, what are the shapes of:

$$\mathbf{X}^T \qquad \mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X} \qquad \mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y} \qquad (\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}$$
:::

::: {.fragment}
- $\mathbf{X}^T$: $p \times n$
- $\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}$: $(p \times n)(n \times p) = p \times p$ — inner $n$'s cancel
- $\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}$: $(p \times n)(n \times 1) = p \times 1$
- $(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{y}$: $(p \times p)(p \times 1) = p \times 1$ —
  one coefficient per predictor. [Dimensions always chain: inner sizes must
  match, outer sizes survive.]{.hl}
:::

# Wrap-Up {.divider background-color="#1b3a5c"}

## The cheat sheet

| You see | You read |
|---|---|
| $n$, $p$ | # observations, # predictors |
| $\mathbf{y}$ vs $x_i$, $\beta$ | bold = length-$n$ vector; plain = other vectors (ISLP rule) |
| $x_{ij}$ | row $i$ (observation), column $j$ (variable) |
| $\hat{\theta}$ | estimate of the unknown $\theta$ |
| $\bar{x}$ | sample mean of $x$ |
| $\mathbf{X}^T$, $\mathbf{A}^{-1}$ | transpose; matrix inverse |
| $\sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ | "is distributed as" a normal |
| $\sum$, $\prod$ | repeated $+$, repeated $\times$ |
| $E[\cdot]$, $\mathrm{Var}(\cdot)$, $\Pr(\cdot \mid \cdot)$ | expectation, variance, conditional probability |
| $I(\cdot)$, $\arg\min$ | indicator (0/1); the minimizing value |
| Greek / Roman | unknown parameter / observed data |

## How to read any equation in this course

1. **Identify the objects** — which symbols are scalars, vectors, matrices?
   (Bold? Capital? Subscripts left over?)
2. **Check the dimensions** — do the shapes chain correctly? What shape is
   the result?
3. **Find the hats** — what's data, what's truth, what's an estimate?
4. **Say it aloud in words** — every equation is a sentence; if you can't
   speak it, you haven't read it yet

::: {.fragment}
[Notation is the interface. Master the interface, and every chapter of ISLP
gets easier.]{.hl} Keep this deck within reach all summer.
:::

::: notes
Pair well with ISLP Section 1's "Notation and Simple Matrix Algebra" —
this deck is a superset with examples.
:::
